channel.title: Narrative Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Narrative Design on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Fallout vs. Arcanum Story States
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Fallout versus Arcanum’s story states.

This is something I’ve mentioned many, many times on here. I always talk about Fallout having three and Arcanum having 27. Most recently, Uh, I did it in a video and Richard to the Argo asked: “Hey Tim, can you do a video breaking down what a 27 act story looks like? You’ve mentioned it a few times now and I can’t quite wrap my head around that number.

item.title: Tone In Video Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about pickling.

I want to talk about how you can pickle things at home to save. Specifically, I want to talk about pickling red onions and pickling garlic.

They’re both so easy to do that a baby could do it, Even a stupid baby. And we’ve all met one or two stupid babies, am I right? I mean, there’s, these babies know object permanence. They only remember things that they love or that they hate. I mean, come on, babies, there’s more up here than just an amygdala.

item.title: Endings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about endings.

Endings are hard. Beginnings are hard too. In fact, I have a whole video about beginnings and I’ll link it below. But this video is going to come out on my birthday. On August 25th, It’s my big 6 birthday. So of course I’m thinking a lot about beginnings and also about endings. One of the things I was thinking about was the first TV show I ever watched that had an ending that hit me really hard. And this was MASH, and I think this particular one happened in the 70s.

item.title: Neutral Factions
item.description:
Hello everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about neutral factions.

I can’t keep talking like that. I want to talk about neutral factions because I think they’re really interesting and it’s a fun Friday, and this is really a talk on narrative design, which I’m not an expert on, but I’m good at coming up with narrative ideas as long as I work with really good narrative designers who can then write way better than I can. And also I want to, you know, admit I know, alignment is over. All the new games coming out are like alignment is so 1980s.

item.title: NPC Reaction Pros And Cons
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction pros and cons.

This comes from a question from badim pakkin, who said: Tim, I was so hoping you could talk about the broader philosophy behind implementing or not implementing NPC triggers, SL reactions. I think that would be even more interesting than just specific implementation examples. He was asking that on a video I did on NPC’s triggering each other and I was giving a lot of examples, and this is certainly something I can talk about and I will. But funnily enough, if I don’t give a lot of examples in these videos, I frequently get comments from people saying: could you give me examples of that could you talk about in your game or in another game where you see something like the mechanic you’re talking about? So that’s why I give a lot of examples, but this is different. This is going to be the philosophy of why you would do it.

item.title: NPCs Triggering Each Other
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC’s triggering each other.

This is in reaction to well a commenter, odesia hh2 TD, saw my NPC reaction video, which I’ll link below, and asked any ideas on how to expand this to make NPC’s trigger reactions between themselves and thus create emergent Behavior. I left a short comment and they replied. I guess the Magic in this case would be picking good triggers that facilitate the emergence of fun events and interactions. So an idea for a fun Friday: brainstorming cool triggers and interaction ideas. That’s this fun Friday, so let’s do it.

item.title: NPC Reactions
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about NPC reaction.

This comes from a question from AliceLittle1865, who asked: can you make a video, maybe on the topic of NPCs and companions? What I mean is how to create character reactions when they interact with the world or not, for example, how all the companions in New Vegas have some kind of reaction when on a mission or part of the map or player choice or faction. The same could be applied to a tabletop RPG. When the DM creates a character that the players have in their party or not, how would that character react to the interactions and development of the story and how do you prepare for that? I want to break this into two parts because the, at least for a video game, the complicated thing is how to set it up so you can do the reaction, and then I’ll talk very briefly on what things you might want to react to. Also, I call this NPC reaction. It is: commonly used for companion reaction, since companions are with you, are following you around and they see a lot of what you do. But I called this NPC reaction because you can really use it for anything in any character in the game who’s not the player. We’ve all played games where the NPCs react to things you do and say: my games all do that. To me that’s one of the just wonderful beautiful things about an RPG is reacting to what the player does and a big chunk of that reaction comes from having non-player characters say: I saw what you did, I saw what you said.

item.title: Story Telling And Player Agency
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about storytelling and player agency.

This is something I’ve touched on in a ton of videos. I will try to link as many as I can below, but there are a lot of them. I wanted to make a specific video on this because I kind of feel like the need to laser focus on the fact that storytelling and player agency are a little contradictory. They’re a little bit at odds and at some point if you want to make a computer role playing game, you need to decide, like consciously, which one of those takes precedence in your game. Now, of course, I have a bias here. I love player agency over all else.

item.title: Sometimes I Hate VO
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how sometimes I hate voiceover, hate.

I hate what it does to me as a game designer: reaches deep into my game design soul and just punches it and nobody acknowledges it and everybody’s like: vo is great, wonderful. Now let me just say before I continue, there are times where I think voice over elevates games.

Trust me, I wrote the intro to Fallout that everybody likes, the one about war never changing, and all that did. I think what I wrote was fantastic, the, a perfect diamond of writing.

item.title: Editing Your Game Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Axion as a hypothetical particle to explain dark matter through their ability to violate CP symmetry in Quantum chromodynamics — wait, wrong Channel.

Today, I want to talk about editing your game story. I’m not a good writer — well, I’m not a writer, especially not a good one. However, I frequently find myself giving feedback to narrative designers because of my ability. My feedback doesn’t take the form of do this, I know better, but instead I give feedback in the form of: I know what you’re trying to do and it’s not coming through now. Sometimes I know what they’re trying to do because I’ve talked to them, or it’s one of the — uh, discussed pillars we had, for here’s what I want the characters to do. Sometimes it’s just I’m playing this character and I’m like this character seems to be trying to be my friend, but they’re just coming across as a sarcastic jerk that I don’t want anywhere near me.

item.title: Writing Evil Characters
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing evil characters.

Specifically, I want to answer a question, a second question, uh, from Nash kruka, who asks: how, where do you find yourself wanting to draw the line for evil options in an RPG? Similarly to how I find myself having a hard time choosing the evil option in a game, I find myself having a hard time writing them? Most games I’ve played have far fewer Bad Karma options than good. Maybe one evil dialogue to the half dozen good dialogue options. How do you weigh in on this? Okay, Nash, I know you’re asking about how to write dialogue for the player where it’s evil. Um, the way I think about this is: how would I write a good evil NPC?

item.title: Writing Good NPCs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing good NPCs.

Now I’m going to start with the big caveat that I’ve said on here many times: I am not a good writer. I’ve tried to be good, I’ve worked on It’s something I can improve past a certain limited amount of. I can write lore text. I can write short narration, but personal one-on-one dialogue — no, I’m not that good at, but I am good at seeing good writing of that category.

item.title: Touchy Subjects
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about touchy subjects.

I like that. This is actually me answering a question from sherner who asks: hey, Tim, I would like to know how do you handle touchy subjects in your games — religion, politics, violence, Etc. How do you know if you’re pushing too hard or going too far on those things? Or even should we bother about not going to those places at all?

item.title: Shopping Cart Theory
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about shopping cart Theory.

This is for narrative game narrative designers or budding game narrative designers out there. It’s just something for you to think about. It’s a very interesting way to think about and frame morality, both in real life and in video games. The shopping cart theory is a very simple philosophical situation that illustrates Ates people’s moralities in a very Stark, clear, simple, clean way. So of course, I love it because it’s simple, but it makes you think things and you realize quickly things aren’t as simple and straightforward as you might think. So what is shopping cart Theory? Basically what this is.

item.title: Using Mechanics For Storytelling
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about using Mechanics for storytelling, and

this is an answer to a question by Benjamin nun 11. Can you talk about gaming gameplay mechanics as storytelling devices? By that I mean where gameplay mechanic is directly reinforcing a character’s connection with the setting or the story.

So, uh, Benjamin, I love this question. It’s the main reason I like to do mechanic after deciding the setting and story, because I think mechanics should support those. Did a video about people who do mechanics.

item.title: Subverting Tropes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about subverting tropes because I did a video on tropes that I think get overused.

I’ll link that below, and dope nasty wanted to know if there were any tropes. I did like, so, okay, in that video I talked about how overuse of tropes bore me. Um, some people called me out and saying, hey, you’ve used those tropes yourself and I’m like, okay, I did. To be clear, well, first of all, the way I’ve done things has shifted over the decades. So, yeah, if you look at a game that’s 20 or 30 years old, you may go, hey, you did that there. I’m like, yeah, and I kind of wish I hadn’t. Um, but to be clear, I also said I, I usually let people get one use of these.

item.title: Designing Mechanics First
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I want to talk about the idea of Designing mechanics first for your game before doing anything else.

This is actually in response to a question from this is fine. Who asks: you’ve mentioned time and again that it goes setting, then story and finally system mechanics. But what about the approach that some directors use, like Miyazaki Ora, to design mechanics, movement levels, ET first and then develop a fitting story based on one, the lore established in the background and two, the things that organically emerge while developing the game itself?

item.title: Canonical Endings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about canonical endings, and by canonical endings I mean the ending of the of a game that is considered to be the right ending, or the correct ending, or the true ending or, at the very minimum, the ending the developer expected you to have.

Now, for nonlinear role playing games, that there’s only one ending. Everybody has the exact same experience and then experiences the one ending that there is, and that’s the canonical ending. But for a lot of my games, which were nonlinear, multiple ending, very choice and consequence-based games, finding the canonical story ending can be tough.

item.title: Grey Morality
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about gray morality.

This has come up a lot in my games. It’s the situation where you feel like you’ve been presented with a number of options to finish a quest and there’s really no right choice, and sometimes there’s no wrong choice either. Both of them just lead to different things happening, none of which affect you or a lot of other people, and sometimes we, you It’s hard to even describe whether there’s a right or wrong choice, because it’s hard to Define what’s right or wrong.

item.title: How To Gate Non-Linear Stories
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to gate nonlinear stories.

This comes from a question from Galaxy 5 production who asks: when you’re programming a game, how do you make sure things happen in sequence according to a story, not only so that a, b and c happen before D, but also maybe events andf can happen at any time between events A and Z, or maybe, based on player decisions, events change, Etc. Interesting question I do have a video already on non-linear stories, but I didn’t really talk about the gating stage or gating events, which does happen. Um, I’ll give examples from Fallout and Arcanum as I go along. The just kind of explain those ideas and how it kind of changed, how I did things over time. But I want to make something clear and it’s probably just the wording you chose: but this kind of gating happens at your design stage.

item.title: Gameplay's Influence On Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game plays: influence on story.

Moji XOXO asks: how much does gameplay influence what stories are or can be told in video games? How do developers grapple with the tension of a story that wants to do one thing or go a certain way, while ensuring that gameplay stays consistently fun and engaging — engaging, throughout? That is a good question. I think I’ve touched on this in other videos a bit, but I’ll just restate it here. It is very, very challenging for video game narrative designers to stop thinking linearly. Most of them are used to writing stories, whether they’re for books or movies or TV shows, and those proceed linearly where games — at least RPG games, which is what I’m talking about here — do not, or mine don’t, and that’s something I want to talk about. So the first red flag to me goes off when I’m having meetings with narrative designers and they say things like: the player goes here: and the player does this and the first thing I say is what if the player goes there and does something else?

item.title: Implementing Endgame Slides
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk to you about implementing endgame slides.

I know I’ve talked about slides before and in a way this is going to be a companion video to the one I did last month on how to do the beginning of video games. But end slides is something that I’ve done in a lot of my games, starting with Fallout. My games are kind of known for having choice and content quence called CNC, which — just a side note — CNC used to refer to the game command and Conquer and for a brief period in the 90s, the CNC Music Factory. That song I got the power very popular in that Simpsons episode where Homer thinks Bart’s gay.

item.title: Story Vs Game Design
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Story versus game design or, as sherner asks, what is more important for a game, story or game design?

I know, ideally there should be a balance between the two, but that is so rare to find. What’s your view on it? Sorry, I got a tickle in my throat today. So let me say first of all, this is a good question, but maybe not for the reason you think I’m going to say it’s a good question.

item.title: Game Lore
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game lore.

I want to answer a question from armored spaz, who asks: how do you keep players informed about the game world, and how could you, how could the game be designed to make the player want to find out more about the game? I think this is a really good question. I almost didn’t answer it though, because it kind of moves into the narrative design territory that I have said in the past, I’m not that good at. But I think I’m good at thinking about narrative design. I’m just not good at implementing narrative design, so let’s talk about that also.

item.title: Villains
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about villains in my appropriately lit villains Lair.

So I’ve done a video about bosses where I talked about bosses being encounters that tested how well the player — was uh, getting at the game using that particular character. But villains are different. Villains are bosses, but all boss bosses are not villains. Villains are the, characters that drive the story, and usually there at the end there’s a encounter with the villain who is a boss. But the villain is the one who made the whole story proceed. So I wanted to talk about what makes a good villain. I’m going to find them, kind of give some examples from my games, and then I want to talk about a villain that I did badly, that I want to redo. So let’s talk about what makes a good villain. I think the best thing about a villain is that their goal is not only believable, you think that they’re actually going to be. Able to achieve this goal — but it’s also justifiable in the sense that it kind of makes sense. It’s kind of something that you could be convinced is a good thing. Maybe the player can even be swayed to join the villain. I think that’s important.

item.title: Fallout: The Original Intro Story
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the original story intro to Fallout.

Let me explain, and I’m kind of excited. So if you watched the video where I’m talking with Scott Campbell, he mentioned that he thought the Vault idea was mine. I thought the Vault idea grew out of our idea for making the game Alien Invasion and that people had hidden in vaults. But he said I remember you coming up with the idea for the vault after the brainstorming and that you even said you dreamed about it.

item.title: Tropes
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about tropes, specifically tropes in computer role-playing games, including the ones

I’ve used and the ones I haven’t used because I hate them. Let’s start with the first one: amnesia. I hate, this Trope so hard. And yes, I will say here what I told Chris Avalon about planescape torment: you get to use Amnesia once. I view Amnesia as kind of a hacky, lazy way of doing the start of an RPG, and I get why people do it. They want to make a character who doesn’t know things, the same way the player doesn’t know things. I think there’s other ways to do it. I’ve done that in my own games.

item.title: Arcanum Generated Dialog
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanum’s generated dialogue system, and I know

I’ve been talking about Arcanum a lot, but I mean I’ve got the code right in front of me. I’ve been going through it a lot, I’ve been playing with it, and the generated dialogue system is something I’m particularly proud of because it let the few designers that were on arcanuman — we had even of the fewer designers, we had even fewer narrative designers — and it let those few people I mean think about. We only had 14 people Tops on Arcanum and just a few writing dialogue. They let them make so much content in the game, which so it was a good tool. But in particular, I liked it because it really doubled down on arcanum’s reactivity, and I’ll talk about why, um, when I get to the NPC one. But this could be really long, I’m probably talking really fast, but there’s just so much to talk about and it’s a detailed topic for me.

item.title: Storytelling in MMORPGs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about storytelling in massively multiplayer online RPGs.

I recently did a podcast — it was really good, should be up in August — and the interviewer uh, who was asking me all kinds of really good questions. I mean, I love when a journalist or anyone interviewing you asked you really evocative questions that make you think, rather than, what was your influence for this game? How did you design this area? What made you think of this feature? I mean, I get it. Maybe you’ve been told to ask those questions, but I’ve had answered those a billion times. So what he asked towards the end of the — uh, I don’t want to call an interview, we just had a discussion for a couple hours. He said: what got away from you? Because I know you’re retiring — what do you think got away from you?

item.title: Humor In Video Games
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about humor in video games, but before I do I want to point out

this is my last shirt. You’ve seen every shirt I own, including shirts that were boxed up in the garage for a decade or two or four. This is it. I’m gonna start repeating shirts after this. A thing didn’t think it would be a thing, but it was a thing. That thing is over.

item.title: Everything I learned from Disneyland
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do something a little different.

All the other videos have been me talking just off the top of my head, but several people have asked in comments about what Inspirations I have for game design and if any of those Inspirations have come not from games or books or movies, but just like everyday activities. And I realized not only do I have just such an inspiration, but I’ve actually talked about this professionally twice, once to the area Design Group I had at carbine, and then again just a couple years ago to, uh, the team on The Outer Worlds 2.. So I wanted to talk to you today about what would be called the top 10 area design roles, or what I instead called Everything I know about RPGs. I learned it, so this could be a little long.
